name: Gravel loop to 'l'Havana Xica' of Garraf
name_original: Vuelta en gravel a “l’Havana Xica” del Garraf
description:
“L’Havana xica” (little Havana) is the nickname by which Vilanova i la Geltrú, the capital of Garraf, was known. This name responds to the strong relationship that was established between this population and the island of Cuba from the 19th century to the beginning of the 20th. Numerous villains traveled to America and specifically to the Caribbean island to make their fortune. They returned converted into "Indians" or "Americans" (pronounced "americanus"), to show their wealth and aesthetic taste through the construction of palaces and monumental estates. This Route offers you a route that does not go far from the center of Vilanova i la Geltrú, from the sea to the hills that surround it, visiting some of these beautiful Indian houses.

You start the Route at the Vilanova i la Geltrú commuter station, to head south following the contour of the coast, to the Prat de Vilanova beach. You can usually skirt this beach by taking the GR 92.5 to the mouth of the Foix River. However, in my last check (November 2023), the section that runs parallel to the train track is impassable due to weeds. Although some cyclists and walkers pass by there, I preferred to take a detour inland and return to the beach a few kilometers later.

Once on the seafront, you continue in a southerly direction until you reach the mouth of the Foix River, which you follow inland. You now head to the highest point of the Route, in the first foothills of the Teixidor mountain range. From here you begin the descent to return to the fields and paths that surround Vilanova i la Geltrú. In this area you have your first contact with the Indian houses, such as Can Cabanyes or the Torre del Veguer.

At the end of the tour you enter the old town of Vilanova i la Geltrú again, where you can admire some buildings such as the Torre de la Inmortalitat. When you pass by Plaça de la Vila, it is worth stopping for a moment to admire Indian constructions such as the Olivella house. This central square of Vilanova i la Geltrú is the authentic nerve center of the Indian constructions of the capital of Garraf. The route ends a few meters further down, in the railway station square.
region_intro: Gravel biking around Vilanova I La Geltrú offers diverse terrain situated between the Mediterranean Sea and rolling hills. The region is characterized by its proximity to three natural parks: Garraf, Foix, and Olèrdola. Landscapes include limestone karst formations, vineyards, forests, and coastal paths, providing varied surfaces for gravel cyclists. This area features a mix of unpaved segments, compact gravel tracks, and some challenging singletracks. Best gravel bike trails around Vilanova I La Geltrú The most popular gravel bike trail is Gravel loop to 'l'Havana Xica' of Garraf, a 21.8 miles (35.0 km) trail that takes 2 hours 51 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the Garraf Natural Park, featuring varied terrain and elevation changes. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Sitges Dirt Road – Sakya Tashi Ling Monastery loop from Baixador de la Font, a difficult 30.2 miles (48.6 km) path. This route includes dirt roads and significant climbs, passing by the Sakya Tashi Ling Monastery. Local gravel bikers also love the View of Pic de l'Àliga – Stone Hut loop from Vilanova i la Geltrú, a 29.2 miles (47.0 km) trail leading through the Garraf Natural Park, often completed in about 3 hours 47 minutes. Gravel biking around Vilanova I La Geltrú is defined by coastal paths, extensive vineyards, and the varied landscapes of the Garraf, Foix, and Olèrdola Natural Parks. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Vilanova I La Geltrú are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from around 15 reviews. More than 400 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Vilanova I La Geltrú's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on gravel bike trails around Vilanova i la Geltrú?
A: The region offers a diverse mix of terrain, ranging from unpaved segments and compact gravel tracks to more challenging singletracks. You'll encounter limestone karst geology in Garraf Natural Park, scenic routes through vineyards, and coastal paths with varying gradients. This variety ensures a dynamic riding experience.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly gravel routes in the area?
A: While many routes are rated moderate to difficult, there are options suitable for beginners. Out of 55 total gravel tours, 3 are classified as easy and 17 as moderate. These typically feature less elevation gain and smoother surfaces, often found through the extensive vineyards or along flatter coastal sections.

Q: What natural parks can I explore by gravel bike near Vilanova i la Geltrú?
A: You can explore three significant natural parks: Garraf, Foix, and Olèrdola. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1359548977/gravel-loop-to-l-havana-xica-of-garraf" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel loop to 'l'Havana Xica' of Garraf&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, takes you deep into Garraf Natural Park, known for its unique karst landscapes and sea views. Foix Natural Park offers picturesque routes around its reservoir and Castellet Castle.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or viewpoints I can see along the gravel trails?
A: Many routes offer stunning views and pass by interesting landmarks. You can climb to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2579854" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puig de l'Àliga&lt;/a&gt; for panoramic vistas of the Penedès plain and Garraf coast. Other highlights include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/440250" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castellet Castle and the Foix Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, and the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5026242" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Trinity Hermitage&lt;/a&gt; along coastal trails.

Q: Are there any circular gravel bike routes available?
A: Yes, many of the gravel routes around Vilanova i la Geltrú are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39539888" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sitges Dirt Road – Sakya Tashi Ling Monastery loop from Baixador de la Font&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging circular path that includes significant climbs and dirt roads.

Q: What do other gravel bikers enjoy most about the trails in Vilanova i la Geltrú?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from around 15 reviews. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, the mix of coastal and mountainous terrain, and the opportunity to explore natural parks and vineyards. The variety of routes catering to different skill levels is also frequently highlighted.

Q: Is it possible to access the gravel trails using public transport?
A: Vilanova i la Geltrú is well-connected by public transport, particularly by train from Barcelona. Many routes start directly from the town, making them accessible without a car. For routes further afield, local bus services might connect to smaller villages near trailheads, though checking specific routes and bike transport policies is advisable.

Q: Are there options for half-day gravel bike rides?
A: Absolutely. Many routes are suitable for half-day adventures, typically ranging from 30 to 50 kilometers. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23746773" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Pic de l'Àliga – Stone Hut loop from Vilanova i la Geltrú&lt;/a&gt; is about 47 km and can be completed in under 4 hours, making it a great half-day option.

Q: What is the best time of year for gravel biking in Vilanova i la Geltrú?
A: The Mediterranean climate makes Vilanova i la Geltrú suitable for gravel biking for much of the year. Spring (April-May) and Autumn (September-October) offer pleasant temperatures and less crowded trails. Summers can be hot, especially inland, so early morning rides are recommended. Winters are generally mild, though some higher elevation trails might be cooler.

Q: Are there any specific permits required for cycling in the natural parks?
A: Generally, no specific permits are required for cycling on designated trails within the natural parks around Vilanova i la Geltrú. However, it's always good practice to respect park regulations, stay on marked paths, and be mindful of other users and wildlife. For detailed information, you can consult the official Garraf Natural Park website or local tourist information.

Q: Can I find cafes or pubs along the gravel routes for a break?
A: Yes, many routes pass through or near charming villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and local establishments. The Penedès region, known for its vineyards, offers many opportunities to stop for refreshments. Planning your route with komoot allows you to identify potential stops along the way.

Q: Are there any challenging gravel routes for experienced riders?
A: Vilanova i la Geltrú is well-suited for experienced riders seeking a challenge. Out of 55 routes, 35 are classified as difficult. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31022648" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Castell de Ribes – Castillo de Ribes loop from Turó del Seu&lt;/a&gt; feature significant elevation changes and technical sections, providing a demanding ride through varied landscapes.
tips: The old road from Vilanova i la Geltrú to Cubelles is an excellent alternative to the very busy C-31 road.

The vineyard huts are also known as dry stone huts, cabins or popularly "mulasses". They are small constructions made by superimposing stones of different sizes and shapes so that no type of cement or mortar was needed.

These buildings are usually located next to vineyards, olive trees or almond trees, since their purpose was to serve as a refuge for farmers, who in times past did not have fast means of transportation and very often had to spend the night in the fields. .

The farmhouse of Can Cabanyes is a house-museum located in the municipality of Vilanova i la Geltrú. It was built in 1798 in the place where the house of Can Parellada was by the merchant Josep Fuster and Llorenç de Cabanyes.

The construction is Italian style, with neoclassical aesthetic criteria. In 1975 the family gave it to the town hall of Vilanova i la Geltrú. It is currently the headquarters of Manuel de Cabanyes' Center for Interpretation of Romanticism.

The Torre del Veguer is a fortified house of medieval origin, located in the municipality of Sant Pere de Ribes.

Its history begins in 1318, when Guillem de Cortei bought the estate, which would become known as Quadra d'Escortei. In the year 1400, the monastery of Sant Jeroni de Montoliver was founded in this place, but years later the monks abandoned it and the house passed into the hands of Gaspar d'Avinyó. In 1601, a descendant of Gaspar d'Avinyó bequeathed the property to his daughter, who married a native of Vilafranca del Penedès, and from this moment on it was called Torre del Veguer, probably because the man from Vilafranca held the position of "veguer." "(representative of the Count of Barcelona in Penedès).

In 1883 the property was acquired by Josep Ferrer i Vidal, who carried out important renovations, following aesthetic guidelines inspired by medieval architecture, preserving original elements such as the semicircular arch access door and the exterior buttresses, as well as semicircular arches. point and ogivals inside.

The Torre de la Inmortalitat, also known as Torre de l'Hort de l'Hospital, is a defensive construction that was built in 1839 during the first Carlist war.

It has an elliptical plan and a truncated conical shape, and is built with irregular stone blocks. The loopholes and battlements can still be distinguished. It is currently part of the courtyard of the Sant Antoni Abat hospital.

The parish church of Sant Antoni Abat is a baroque and neoclassical style temple. The original temple dates back to the 14th century, and in the 17th century it was agreed to build a new church, starting with a new bell tower, separate from the original building.

The works were delayed by the War of Succession, and were not finished until 1879. It would undergo even more modifications, due to the damage suffered during the civil war. Finally, in 1977 the façade facing Plaza de les Neus was modified.

The square is home to the Town Hall. It is connected to the main pedestrian streets and functions as an administrative center and meeting place.
The floor is decorated with a Portuguese-style mosaic that forms black and white geometric patterns on the surface.
